name: Stowell's Evergreen
description: A large white sweet corn from the 1840s, producing 8–9 inch ears with creamy white kernels and an exceptionally sweet, rich flavor. One of the longest-popular sweet corn varieties in American garden history.
history: Introduced by Nathan Stowell of Burlington, New Jersey around 1848. The 'Evergreen' name refers to its ability to hold its eating quality longer on the stalk than other varieties of its time — a revolutionary trait for 19th-century growers.
seasonal_quirks: Long season requires a full summer of heat. Better choice for warmer climates or long-season regions. The ears hold their quality on the stalk slightly longer than many heirlooms — giving a day or two of harvest flexibility.
harvest_cues: Large, full ears with tight, milky white kernels. Silks fully brown. Husks still green and tight. Kernels should feel full and round.
variety_notes: Favored for cream-style corn and corn chowder due to its high sugar and starch balance. Stalks are tall — up to 9 feet — and need wind protection or they will lodge.
preservation: Excellent cream-style canned or frozen. Whole ear freezes well blanched.
